title: Vehicle monocoque body and method of manufacturing thereof
abstract: In one embodiment there is disclosed a vehicle monocoque body formed without a chassis, comprising: a support surface (10) having a front end and a rear end; and is adapted for providing a base for the vehicle and for direct coupling of axles for attachment of wheels; a top surface (11) adapted as a roof for the vehicle; opposing lateral surfaces (12,14) extending upwardly from the support surface (10); a front surface (16) extending upwardly from the front end of the support surface (10) and; a rear surface (18) extending upwardly from the rear end of the support surface (10). The support surface (10), the top surface (11), the opposing lateral surfaces (12,14), the front and rear surfaces (16,18) are formed from a composite material comprising a combination of least two materials comprising of kevlar, carbon and glass fibre materials; whereby the surfaces are joined together to form the vehicle monocoque body (1).
claims: Claims CLAIMS: 1. A vehicle monocoque body (1) without a chassis, comprising: a support surface (10) having a front end and a rear end; and is adapted for providing a base for the vehicle and for direct coupling of axles for attachment of wheels; a top surface (1 1) adapted as a roof for the vehicle; opposing lateral surfaces (12,14) extending upwardly from the support surface (10); a front surface (16) extending upwardly from the front end of the support surface (10) and; a rear surface (18) extending upwardly from the rear end of the support surface (10); characterised in that; the support surface (10), the top surface (1 1), the opposing lateral surfaces (12,14), the front and rear surfaces (16,18) are formed from a composite material comprising a combination of kevlar, carbon and glass fibre materials; whereby the surfaces are joined together to form the vehicle monocoque body (1). 2. The vehicle monocoque body ( 1 ) as claimed in Claim 1 , wherein the composite further comprises Carbon material. 3. The vehicle monocoque body (1) as claimed in Claim 1, wherein the surfaces are formed based on variable thicknesses to accommodate variable loads. 4. The vehicle monocoque body (1) as claimed in Claim 1, wherein the body is formed with the composite material such that the load of the vehicle is spread proportionately across the vehicle monocoque body (1). 5. The vehicle monocoque body (1) as claimed in Claim 1 , wherein the composite material further includes a filler material to provide at least one gap between the kevlar, carbon or glass fibre materials to increase the load bearing capacity without increasing the overall weight. 6. The vehicle monocoque body (1) as claimed in Claim 1, wherein the filler material is a foam layer to provide a gap between the kevlar, carbon or glass fibre material to increase the load bearing capacity of the vehicle. 7. The vehicle monocoque body (1) as claimed in Claim 1 , wherein the combination of at least two of the materials comprising: kevlar, carbon and glass fibre materials is adapted as skin layers for the composite material. 8. The vehicle monocoque body (1) as claimed in Claim 2, wherein the foam material forms the core layer for the composite material; hence sandwiched between the skin layers of the composite material. 9. The vehicle monocoque body (1) as claimed in Claim 1, wherein the axle further includes an axle support means. 10. The vehicle monocoque body (1) as claimed in Claim 1, wherein the vehicle is a bus powered by electrical motors. 1 1. A method of fabricating a composite material to be used in constructing a monocoque body of a vehicle comprising: providing at least two skin layers formed from a combination of at least two materials from a group of materials comprising: kevlar, carbon and glass fibre materials; wherein the kevlar, carbon and glass fibre materials are prepared in a manner such that they form a matrix structure; providing a filler material to form a gap between the skin layers; arranging the layers in a manner such that the skin layers are sandwiching the filler material; bonding the layers together to form the composite material. 12. The method as claimed in Claim 9, wherein bonding the layers includes subjecting the layers to resin impregnation process and a curing process thereafter. 13. A method of manufacturing a vehicle having a carrying capacity of 10 tonnes and above, comprising: preparing a composite material from a combination of at least two materials from a group comprising: kevlar, carbon and fibre glass materials; forming a vehicle body based on the composite material; and securing a plurality of axles to the vehicle body for attachment of wheels. 14. Using the composite material as claimed in Claim 1 to construct a vehicle body. 15. The method as claimed in Claim 1, wherein the vehicle is a bus powered by electrical motors.
